claim: DeepSeek V4 Pro exhibits 'tool confusion' where it sends malformed tool-call schemas and repeats the same error 50+ times on average even when sent Zod validation errors.
actionable: Implement a deterministic repair layer: catch malformed tool outputs (null, empty object, wrong type), fix them automatically, then send a repair hint back to explain the correction.
source_title: Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste

claim: Open models trained on high-quality data show learned behavior of 'trusting themselves' and ignoring correction feedback, causing repeated tool-call errors.
actionable: When using open models, implement repair logic rather than relying on error feedback alone; pair repairs with educational hints so the model learns the correction.
source_title: Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste

claim: DeepSeek V4 Flash and Kimi both show identical tool-calling failure patterns across billions of tokens; repair logic fixes ~16,000 variations of these errors.
actionable: Build a generalized repair library cataloging common tool-call failures by model; apply repairs deterministically across error types to improve any open model's reliability.
source_title: Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste

claim: Applying repair logic enables DeepSeek V4 Flash to go from 'practically completely not useful' to competing with premium models like Opus 4.7.
actionable: Integrate a tool-call repair layer into any coding agent harness; test your open model performance metrics before and after to quantify improvement.
source_title: Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste

claim: Tool-calling errors in open models are often contract/harness issues rather than true capability gaps; fixable with targeted repairs and semantic hints.
actionable: Before concluding an open model is weak at coding, audit your harness for tool-call failures; implement repair logic and re-evaluate performance.
source_title: Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste

claim: Taste learns coding preferences automatically on a per-repository basis by observing edits/accepts/rejects, building a library of micro-decisions rather than broad rules.
actionable: Enable automatic taste learning in your coding agent by tracking user edits and merges; extract patterns like 'uses pnpm for installs but npm for linking' into taste files.
source_title: Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste

claim: Using OKLCH color space in design prompts improves LLM color-palette control and consistency compared to HSL or other color spaces.
actionable: When prompting for design generation, explicitly instruct models to use OKLCH for color definitions instead of HSL; include OKLCH in design skill/taste files.
source_title: Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste

claim: Seven design patterns (surface-area types like 'monitor', 'compare') guide LLM design decisions; providing this framework reduces design slop by ~90%.
actionable: Create a design skill defining surface-area patterns and intent (e.g., 'Is this a monitoring dashboard? A comparison view?'); include these 7 patterns in design prompts.
source_title: Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste

claim: Permissions/access-control blocks in coding agents slow inference and steer models wrongly; removing permission delays improves creativity and exploration.
actionable: Test your coding agent with and without permission-gate delays; if sluggish behavior correlates with permission prompts, consider safer permission architectures.
source_title: Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste

claim: Taste files are stored in Git repositories and automatically managed; humans write grandiose rules but Taste learns micro-decisions from continuous work.
actionable: Set up automatic taste learning; start with broad rules in taste files but let your agent also track and suggest micro-patterns observed during your workflow.
source_title: Making DeepSeek v4 outperform Opus 4.7 with Taste
